content.content:
---
title: Local Development
product: vercel
url: /docs/microfrontends/local-development
type: conceptual
prerequisites:
  - /docs/microfrontends
related:
  - /docs/microfrontends/quickstart
  - /docs/deployment-protection
  - /docs/deployment-protection/methods-to-bypass-deployment-protection/protection-bypass-automation
summary: Learn about local development on Vercel.
install_vercel_plugin: npx plugins add vercel/vercel-plugin
---

# Microfrontends local development

To provide a seamless local development experience, `@vercel/microfrontends` provides a microfrontends aware local development proxy to run alongside your development servers. This proxy allows you to only run a single microfrontend locally while making sure that all microfrontend requests still work.

## The need for a microfrontends proxy

Microfrontends allow teams to split apart an application and only run an individual microfrontend to improve developer velocity. A downside of this approach is that requests to the other microfrontends won't work unless that microfrontend is also running locally. The microfrontends proxy solves this by intelligently falling back to route microfrontend requests to production for those applications that are not running locally.

For example, if you have two microfrontends `web` and `docs`:

```json filename="microfrontends.json"
{
  "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/microfrontends.json",
  "applications": {
    "web": {
      "development": {
        "fallback": "vercel.com"
      }
    },
    "docs": {
      "routing": [
        {
          "paths": ["/docs/:path*"]
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

A developer working on `/docs` only runs the **Docs** microfrontend, while a developer working on `/blog` only runs the **Web** microfrontend. If a **Docs** developer wants to test a transition between `/docs` and `/blog` , they need to run both microfrontends locally. This is not the case with the microfrontends proxy as it routes requests to `/blog` to the instance of **Web** that is running in production.

Therefore, the microfrontends proxy allows developers to run only the microfrontend they are working on locally and be able to test paths in other microfrontends.

> **⚠️ Warning:** When developing locally with Next.js any traffic a child application receives
> will be redirected to the local proxy. Setting the environment variable
> `MFE_DISABLE_LOCAL_PROXY_REWRITE=1` will disable the redirect and allow you to
> visit the child application directly.

## Setting up microfrontends proxy

### Prerequisites

- Set up your [microfrontends on Vercel](/docs/microfrontends/quickstart)
- All applications that are part of the microfrontend have `@vercel/microfrontends` listed as a dependency
- Optional: [Turborepo](https://turborepo.com) in your repository

- ### Application setup
  In order for the local proxy to redirect traffic correctly, it needs to know which port each application's development server will be using. To keep the development server and the local proxy in sync, you can use the `microfrontends port` command provided by `@vercel/microfrontends` which will automatically assign a port.
  ```json {4} filename="package.json"
  {
    "name": "web",
    "scripts": {
      "dev": "next --port $(microfrontends port)"
    },
    "dependencies": {
      "@vercel/microfrontends": "latest"
    }
  }
  ```
  If you would like to use a specific port for each application, you may configure that in `microfrontends.json`:
  ```json {11-15} filename="microfrontends.json"
  {
    "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/microfrontends.json",
    "applications": {
      "web": {},
      "docs": {
        "routing": [
          {
            "paths": ["/docs/:path*"]
          }
        ],
        "development": {
          "task": "start",
          "local": 3001
        }
      }
    }
  }
  ```
  The `local` field may also contain a host or protocol (for example, `my.special.localhost.com:3001` or `https://my.localhost.com:3030`).

  If the name of the application in `microfrontends.json` (such as `web` or `docs`) does not match the name used in `package.json`, you can also set the `packageName` field for the application so that the local development proxy knows if the application is running locally.
  ```json {11} filename="microfrontends.json"
  {
    "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/microfrontends.json",
    "applications": {
      "web": {},
      "docs": {
        "routing": [
          {
            "paths": ["/docs/:path*"]
          }
        ],
        "packageName": "my-docs-package"
      }
    }
  }
  ```
  ```json {2} filename="package.json"
  {
    "name": "my-docs-package",
    "scripts": {
      "dev": "next --port $(microfrontends port)"
    },
    "dependencies": {
      "@vercel/microfrontends": "latest"
    }
  }
  ```

- ### Starting local proxy
  The local proxy is started automatically when running a microfrontend development task with `turbo`. By default a microfrontend application's `dev` script is selected as the development task, but this can be changed with the `task` field in `microfrontends.json`.

  Running `turbo web#dev` will start the `web` microfrontends development server along with a local proxy that routes all requests for `docs` to the configured production host.
  > **💡 Note:** This requires version `2.3.6` or `2.4.2` or newer of the `turbo` package.

- ### Setting up your monorepo
  - ### Option 1: Adding Turborepo to a monorepo
    Turborepo is the suggested way to work with microfrontends as it provides a managed way for running multiple applications and a proxy simultaneously.

    If you don't already use [Turborepo](https://turborepo.com) in your monorepo, `turbo` can infer a configuration from your `microfrontends.json`. This allows you to start using Turborepo in your monorepo without any additional configuration.

    To get started, follow the [Installing `turbo`](https://turborepo.com/docs/getting-started/installation#installing-turbo) guide.

    Once you have installed `turbo`, run your development tasks using `turbo` instead of your package manager. This will start the local proxy alongside the development server.

    You can start the development task for the **Web** microfrontend by running `turbo run dev --filter=web`. Review Turborepo's [filter documentation](https://turborepo.com/docs/reference/run#--filter-string) for details about filtering tasks.

    For more information on adding Turborepo to your repository, review [adding Turborepo to an existing repository](https://turborepo.com/docs/getting-started/add-to-existing-repository).
  - ### Option 2: Using without Turborepo
    If you do not want to use Turborepo, you can invoke the proxy directly.
    ```json {5} filename="package.json"
    {
      "name": "web",
      "scripts": {
        "dev": "next --port $(microfrontends port)",
        "proxy": "microfrontends proxy microfrontends.json --local-apps web"
      },
      "dependencies": {
        "@vercel/microfrontends": "latest"
      }
    }
    ```
    Review [Understanding the proxy command](#understanding-the-proxy-command) for more details.

- ### Accessing the microfrontends proxy
  When testing locally, you should use the port from the microfrontends proxy to test your application. For example, if `docs` runs on port `3001` and the microfrontends proxy is on port `3024`, you should visit `http://localhost:3024/docs` to test all parts of their application.

  You can change the port of the local development proxy by setting `options.localProxyPort` in `microfrontends.json`:
  ```json {6} filename="microfrontends.json"
  {
    "applications": {
      // ...
    },
    "options": {
      "localProxyPort": 4001
    }
  }
  ```

## Debug routing

To debug issues with microfrontends locally, enable microfrontends debug mode when running your application. Details about changes to your application, such as environment variables and rewrites, will be printed to the console. If using the [local development proxy](/docs/microfrontends/local-development), the logs will also print the name of the application and URL of the destination where each request was routed to.

1. Set an environment variable `MFE_DEBUG=1`
2. Or, set `debug` to `true` when calling `withMicrofrontends`

## Polyrepo setup

If you're working with a polyrepo setup where microfrontends are distributed across separate repositories, you'll need additional configuration since the `microfrontends.json` file won't be automatically detected.

### Accessing the configuration file

First, ensure that each microfrontend repository has access to the shared configuration:

- **Option 1: Use the Vercel CLI** to fetch the configuration:

  ```bash
  vercel microfrontends pull
  ```

  This command will download the `microfrontends.json` file from your default application to your local repository.

  If you haven't linked your project yet, the command will prompt you to [link your project to Vercel](https://vercel.com/docs/cli/project-linking) first.

> **💡 Note:** This command requires the Vercel CLI 44.2.2 to be installed.

- **Option 2: Set the `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG` environment variable** with a path pointing to your `microfrontends.json` file:

  ```bash
  export VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG=/path/to/microfrontends.json
  ```

  You can also add this to your `.env` file:

  ```bash filename=".env"
  VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG=/path/to/microfrontends.json
  ```

### Running the local development proxy

In a polyrepo setup, you'll need to start each microfrontend application separately since they're in different repositories. Unlike monorepos where Turborepo can manage multiple applications, polyrepos require manual coordination:

- ### Start your local microfrontend application
  Start your microfrontend application with the proper port configuration. Follow the [Application setup](/docs/microfrontends/local-development#application-setup) instructions to configure your development script with the `microfrontends port` command.

- ### Run the microfrontends proxy
  In the same or a separate terminal, start the microfrontends proxy:
  ```bash
  microfrontends proxy --local-apps your-app-name
  ```
  Make sure to specify the correct application name that matches your `microfrontends.json` configuration.

- ### Access your application
  Visit the proxy URL shown in the terminal output (typically `http://localhost:3024`) to test the full microfrontends experience. This URL will route requests to your local app or production fallbacks as configured.

Since you're working across separate repositories, you'll need to manually start any other microfrontends you want to test locally, each in their respective repository.

## Understanding the proxy command

When setting up your monorepo without turborepo, the `proxy` command used inside the `package.json` scripts has the following specifications:

- `microfrontends` is an executable provided by the `@vercel/microfrontends` package.
  - You can also run it with a command like `npm exec microfrontends ...` (or the equivalent for your package manager), as long as it's from a context where the `@vercel/microfrontends` package is installed.
- `proxy` is a sub-command to run the local proxy.
- `microfrontends.json` is the path to your microfrontends configuration file. If you have a monorepo, you may also leave this out and the script will attempt to locate the file automatically.
- `--local-apps` is followed by a space separated list of the applications running locally. For the applications provided in this list, the local proxy will route requests to those local applications. Requests for other applications will be routed to the `fallback` URL specified in your microfrontends configuration for that app.

For example, if you are running the **Web** and **Docs** microfrontends locally, this command would set up the local proxy to route requests locally for those applications, and requests for the remaining applications to their fallbacks:

```json filename="package.json"
microfrontends proxy microfrontends.json --local-apps web docs
```

We recommend having a proxy command associated with each application in your microfrontends group. For example:

- If you run `npm run docs-dev` to start up your `docs` application for local development, set up `npm run docs-proxy` as well
  - This should pass `--local-apps docs` so it sends requests to the local `docs` application, and everything else to the fallback.

Therefore, you can run `npm run docs-dev` and `npm run docs-proxy` to get the full microfrontends setup running locally.

## Falling back to protected deployments

To fall back to a Vercel deployment protected with [Deployment Protection](/docs/deployment-protection), set an environment variable with the value of the [Protection Bypass for Automation](/docs/deployment-protection/methods-to-bypass-deployment-protection/protection-bypass-automation).

You must name the environment variable `AUTOMATION_BYPASS_<transformed app name>`. The name is transformed to be uppercase, and any non letter or number is replaced with an underscore.

For example, the env var name for an app named `my-docs-app` would be:
`AUTOMATION_BYPASS_MY_DOCS_APP`.

### Set the protection bypass environment variable

- ### Enable the Protection Bypass for Automation for your project
  1. Navigate to the Vercel **project for the protected fallback deployment**
  2. Click on the **Settings** tab
  3. Click on **Deployment Protection**
  4. If not enabled, create a new [Protection Bypass for Automation](/docs/deployment-protection/methods-to-bypass-deployment-protection/protection-bypass-automation)
  5. Copy the value of the secret

- ### Set the environment variable in the default app project
  1. Navigate to the Vercel project for the **default application** (may or may not be the same project)
  2. Click on the **Settings** tab
  3. Click on **Environment Variables**
  4. Add a new variable with the name `AUTOMATION_BYPASS_<transformed app name>` (e.g. `AUTOMATION_BYPASS_MY_DOCS_APP`) and the value of the secret from the previous step
  5. Set the selected environments for the variable to `Development`
  6. Click on **Save**

- ### Import the secret using vc env pull
  1. Ensure you have [vc](https://vercel.com/cli) installed
  2. Navigate to the root of the default app folder
  3. Run `vc login` to authenticate with Vercel
  4. Run `vc link` to link the folder to the Vercel project
  5. Run `vc env pull` to pull the secret into your local environment

- ### Update your README.md
  Include [the previous step](#import-the-secret-using-vc-env-pull) in your repository setup instructions, so that other users will also have the secret available.

# Microfrontends local development

To provide a seamless local development experience, `@vercel/microfrontends` provides a microfrontends aware local development proxy to run alongside your development servers. This proxy allows you to only run a single microfrontend locally while making sure that all microfrontend requests still work.

## The need for a microfrontends proxy

Microfrontends allow teams to split apart an application and only run an individual microfrontend to improve developer velocity. A downside of this approach is that requests to the other microfrontends won't work unless that microfrontend is also running locally. The microfrontends proxy solves this by intelligently falling back to route microfrontend requests to production for those applications that are not running locally.

For example, if you have two microfrontends `web` and `docs`:

```json filename="microfrontends.json"
{
  "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/microfrontends.json",
  "applications": {
    "web": {
      "development": {
        "fallback": "vercel.com"
      }
    },
    "docs": {
      "routing": [
        {
          "paths": ["/docs/:path*"]
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

A developer working on `/docs` only runs the **Docs** microfrontend, while a developer working on `/blog` only runs the **Web** microfrontend. If a **Docs** developer wants to test a transition between `/docs` and `/blog` , they need to run both microfrontends locally. This is not the case with the microfrontends proxy as it routes requests to `/blog` to the instance of **Web** that is running in production.

Therefore, the microfrontends proxy allows developers to run only the microfrontend they are working on locally and be able to test paths in other microfrontends.

> **⚠️ Warning:** When developing locally with Next.js any traffic a child application receives
> will be redirected to the local proxy. Setting the environment variable
> `MFE_DISABLE_LOCAL_PROXY_REWRITE=1` will disable the redirect and allow you to
> visit the child application directly.

## Setting up microfrontends proxy

### Prerequisites

- Set up your [microfrontends on Vercel](/docs/microfrontends/quickstart)
- All applications that are part of the microfrontend have `@vercel/microfrontends` listed as a dependency
- Optional: [Turborepo](https://turborepo.com) in your repository

- ### Application setup
  In order for the local proxy to redirect traffic correctly, it needs to know which port each application's development server will be using. To keep the development server and the local proxy in sync, you can use the `microfrontends port` command provided by `@vercel/microfrontends` which will automatically assign a port.
  ```json {4} filename="package.json"
  {
    "name": "web",
    "scripts": {
      "dev": "next --port $(microfrontends port)"
    },
    "dependencies": {
      "@vercel/microfrontends": "latest"
    }
  }
  ```
  If you would like to use a specific port for each application, you may configure that in `microfrontends.json`:
  ```json {11-15} filename="microfrontends.json"
  {
    "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/microfrontends.json",
    "applications": {
      "web": {},
      "docs": {
        "routing": [
          {
            "paths": ["/docs/:path*"]
          }
        ],
        "development": {
          "task": "start",
          "local": 3001
        }
      }
    }
  }
  ```
  The `local` field may also contain a host or protocol (for example, `my.special.localhost.com:3001` or `https://my.localhost.com:3030`).

  If the name of the application in `microfrontends.json` (such as `web` or `docs`) does not match the name used in `package.json`, you can also set the `packageName` field for the application so that the local development proxy knows if the application is running locally.
  ```json {11} filename="microfrontends.json"
  {
    "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/microfrontends.json",
    "applications": {
      "web": {},
      "docs": {
        "routing": [
          {
            "paths": ["/docs/:path*"]
          }
        ],
        "packageName": "my-docs-package"
      }
    }
  }
  ```
  ```json {2} filename="package.json"
  {
    "name": "my-docs-package",
    "scripts": {
      "dev": "next --port $(microfrontends port)"
    },
    "dependencies": {
      "@vercel/microfrontends": "latest"
    }
  }
  ```

- ### Starting local proxy
  The local proxy is started automatically when running a microfrontend development task with `turbo`. By default a microfrontend application's `dev` script is selected as the development task, but this can be changed with the `task` field in `microfrontends.json`.

  Running `turbo web#dev` will start the `web` microfrontends development server along with a local proxy that routes all requests for `docs` to the configured production host.
  > **💡 Note:** This requires version `2.3.6` or `2.4.2` or newer of the `turbo` package.

- ### Setting up your monorepo
  - ### Option 1: Adding Turborepo to a monorepo
    Turborepo is the suggested way to work with microfrontends as it provides a managed way for running multiple applications and a proxy simultaneously.

    If you don't already use [Turborepo](https://turborepo.com) in your monorepo, `turbo` can infer a configuration from your `microfrontends.json`. This allows you to start using Turborepo in your monorepo without any additional configuration.

    To get started, follow the [Installing `turbo`](https://turborepo.com/docs/getting-started/installation#installing-turbo) guide.

    Once you have installed `turbo`, run your development tasks using `turbo` instead of your package manager. This will start the local proxy alongside the development server.

    You can start the development task for the **Web** microfrontend by running `turbo run dev --filter=web`. Review Turborepo's [filter documentation](https://turborepo.com/docs/reference/run#--filter-string) for details about filtering tasks.

    For more information on adding Turborepo to your repository, review [adding Turborepo to an existing repository](https://turborepo.com/docs/getting-started/add-to-existing-repository).
  - ### Option 2: Using without Turborepo
    If you do not want to use Turborepo, you can invoke the proxy directly.
    ```json {5} filename="package.json"
    {
      "name": "web",
      "scripts": {
        "dev": "next --port $(microfrontends port)",
        "proxy": "microfrontends proxy microfrontends.json --local-apps web"
      },
      "dependencies": {
        "@vercel/microfrontends": "latest"
      }
    }
    ```
    Review [Understanding the proxy command](#understanding-the-proxy-command) for more details.

- ### Accessing the microfrontends proxy
  When testing locally, you should use the port from the microfrontends proxy to test your application. For example, if `docs` runs on port `3001` and the microfrontends proxy is on port `3024`, you should visit `http://localhost:3024/docs` to test all parts of their application.

  You can change the port of the local development proxy by setting `options.localProxyPort` in `microfrontends.json`:
  ```json {6} filename="microfrontends.json"
  {
    "applications": {
      // ...
    },
    "options": {
      "localProxyPort": 4001
    }
  }
  ```

## Debug routing

To debug issues with microfrontends locally, enable microfrontends debug mode when running your application. Details about changes to your application, such as environment variables and rewrites, will be printed to the console. If using the [local development proxy](/docs/microfrontends/local-development), the logs will also print the name of the application and URL of the destination where each request was routed to.

content:
read: https://vercel.com/docs/microfrontends/configuration too

we want to use microfrontends proxy 100%. we use MFE for our local dev. 

i need you to clarify understnad using AskUserQuestions

content.text:
OK — read the docs page you linked plus `vercel.com/docs/microfrontends/local-development` and the shipped JSON schema. Here's what they actually say.

**Schema (verbatim) for `child.development.local`:**

> "A local port number or host that this application runs on when it is running locally. If passing a string, include the protocol (optional), host (required) and port (optional). Examples of valid values: 8080, my.localhost.me, my.localhost.me:8080, https://my.localhost.me, https://my.localhost.me:8080. **The default value is `http://localhost:<port>` where port is a stable, unique port number (based on the application name).**"

Two things this confirms:
1. We can put `https://lightfast-app.localhost:1355` (a portless URL) directly in `local`. That's how `local` is *supposed* to be used for non-loopback setups.
2. If we omit `local` entirely, MFE auto-derives a deterministic port from the app name. That's how `apps/www/package.json:16`'s `next dev --port $(microfrontends port)` works today — `lightfast-www` has no `local` so MFE hashes the name to pick a stable port.

**The bit I actually missed (verbatim, local-development docs):**

> "If you're using Turborepo, define the environment variable [`VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG_FILE_NAME`] **outside** of the Turbo invocation when running `turbo dev`, so the local proxy can detect and use the correct configuration file."

So Vercel's *own* documented multi-config pattern is: set the config-filename env var outside the turbo invocation. That outside-of-turbo wrapper layer is literally what `scripts/dev-worktree.sh` would be. The script isn't exotic — it's the documented Vercel pattern for swapping config files at dev time.

**The honest summary, after reading everything:**

- Portless: handles port allocation per worktree via `PORT` injection. ✓
- MFE `local` field: accepts portless hostnames. ✓
- MFE proxy listen port: handled via `MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT` (or wrap the proxy itself in portless). ✓
- **MFE upstream URLs in sibling worktrees**: need to be `<branch>.lightfast-app.localhost` instead of `lightfast-app.localhost`. The branch is in `PORTLESS_URL`. *Something* has to compose the prefixed URL.

The "something" can be:

1. **Vercel-documented pattern**: a wrapper that swaps `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG_FILE_NAME` (or `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG`) outside turbo. ~20 lines, blessed by docs.
2. **Drop MFE proxy in dev, use Next.js rewrites**: each app wrapped in portless individually; `apps/app/next.config.ts` reads `PORTLESS_URL`, derives prefix, generates rewrites for www paths from `microfrontends.json`. No script. Diverges from the proxy emulator but still consumes the same `microfrontends.json` config.
3. **Single-stack-per-worktree-but-fallback**: commit a `microfrontends.json` with `local: "https://lightfast-app.localhost:1355"` (no prefix), only run `dev:full` in one worktree at a time, sibling worktrees use the `fallback` URL (production) for cross-app paths. No script. No concurrent multi-worktree `dev:full`.

Option (1) IS the Vercel-documented approach. Option (2) is genuinely simpler if we accept losing the proxy emulator. Option (3) drops the concurrent requirement.

Which constraint do you want to hold? Concurrent multi-worktree `dev:full`, or no script?

input.prompt: Extract the COMPLETE configuration reference. Every top-level key (applications, options, etc.). Every nested field under applications.* (development, routing, packageName, assetPrefix, etc.). For development: every sub-field (local, fallback, task, port, etc.). For options: every sub-field (localProxyPort, etc.). Every documented default. Every accepted value type and shape. List all environment variables mentioned (VC_MICROFRONTENDS_*, MFE_*). Quote verbatim where definitions or behaviors are described. Do not summarize — I need the full surface.

lastPrompt: read: https://vercel.com/docs/microfrontends/configuration too  we want to use microfrontends proxy 100%. we use MFE for our local dev.   i need you to clarify understnad using AskUserQuestions

content.text:
Read the full config docs. The Schema section on that page is rendered from the JSON schema I already extracted from `node_modules` (same surface). The only configuration pattern Vercel documents for swapping configs at dev time is `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG_FILE_NAME="..." turbo dev` — explicitly outside the turbo invocation.

Given your "100% MFE proxy" + "concurrent multi-worktree" constraints, I want to make sure I understand the actual requirement before proposing again.

questions.question: What's the actual concurrency requirement for worktree dev?

options.label: Full dev:full in N worktrees at once
options.description: Both worktrees run the complete app+www+platform stack simultaneously, each on its own *.lightfast.localhost subdomain. This is the only case that forces per-worktree MFE config injection (and thus the wrapper).

options.label: Single-app dev:* in N worktrees at once
options.description: Worktrees can each run any single app (dev:app, dev:www, dev:platform) concurrently, but only one worktree runs the full stack. Portless handles single-app PORT allocation natively — no script needed.

options.label: One worktree at a time, full stack
options.description: Sibling worktrees exist for code review/branch switching but only one worktree's dev stack runs at any given time. Simplest possible setup; no port-collision avoidance needed.

questions.question: How should `local` ports/hosts be specified in microfrontends.json?

options.label: Keep port numbers (current)
options.description: microfrontends.json keeps `local: 4107` / `local: 4101`. MFE proxy forwards to 127.0.0.1:port directly. Portless wraps the MFE proxy itself and the platform service, but app/www bind raw ports. Per-worktree concurrency requires offset arithmetic on those numbers.

options.label: Switch to portless URLs
options.description: microfrontends.json uses `local: "https://lightfast-app.localhost:1355"`. Proxy forwards through portless. Each app gets its own portless wrap. Branch prefix has to be injected per-worktree (still requires config swap).

options.label: Omit `local` (use MFE name-hash defaults)
options.description: Drop `local` entirely; MFE auto-derives a stable port from app name (the same default `microfrontends port` returns today for lightfast-www). Apps bind via `next dev --port $(microfrontends port)`. Same hash across worktrees → still collides without offset injection.

questions.question: If config injection IS needed, what shape do you want?

options.label: Wrapper script outside turbo
options.description: scripts/dev-worktree.sh sets `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG_FILE_NAME` (or VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG) before turbo. This is verbatim Vercel's documented multi-config pattern: `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG_FILE_NAME="..." turbo dev`. ~20 lines.

options.label: Inline in package.json scripts
options.description: Compose the env vars + JSON config inline in each `dev:*` script using shell. No separate file; uglier readability; same logic.

options.label: Per-developer manual setup
options.description: Each developer creates their own microfrontends-<myname>.json (gitignored) and exports VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG_FILE_NAME in their shell rc. Zero scripts in the repo; manual one-time setup per machine/worktree.

options.label: No injection — concurrency requirement was wrong
options.description: Selecting this means we don't actually need concurrent dev:full, and the wrapper/injection question is moot. (Equivalent to picking option 2 or 3 in Q1.)

input.description: Inspect portless CLI surface and current state

content.content:
portless - Replace port numbers with stable, named .localhost URLs. For humans and agents.

Eliminates port conflicts, memorizing port numbers, and cookie/storage
clashes by giving each dev server a stable .localhost URL.

Install:
  npm install -g portless          Global (recommended)
  npm install -D portless          Project dev dependency

Usage:
  portless proxy start             Start the proxy (HTTPS on port 443, daemon)
  portless proxy start --no-tls    Start without HTTPS (port 80)
  portless proxy start --lan       Start in LAN mode (mDNS for real device testing)
  portless proxy start -p 1355     Start on a custom port (no sudo)
  portless proxy stop              Stop the proxy
  portless <name> <cmd>            Run your app through the proxy
  portless run <cmd>               Infer name from project, run through proxy
  portless get <name>              Print URL for a service (for cross-service refs)
  portless alias <name> <port>     Register a static route (e.g. for Docker)
  portless alias --remove <name>   Remove a static route
  portless list                    Show active routes
  portless trust                   Add local CA to system trust store
  portless clean                   Remove portless state, trust entry, and hosts block
  portless hosts sync              Add routes to /etc/hosts (fixes Safari)
  portless hosts clean             Remove portless entries from /etc/hosts

Examples:
  portless proxy start                # Start HTTPS proxy on port 443
  portless proxy start --no-tls       # Start HTTP proxy on port 80
  portless myapp next dev             # -> https://myapp.localhost
  portless myapp vite dev             # -> https://myapp.localhost
  portless api.myapp pnpm start       # -> https://api.myapp.localhost
  portless run next dev               # -> https://<project>.localhost
  portless run next dev               # in worktree -> https://<worktree>.<project>.localhost
  portless get backend                 # -> https://backend.localhost (for cross-service refs)
  # Wildcard subdomains: tenant.myapp.localhost also routes to myapp

In package.json:
  {
    "scripts": {
      "dev": "portless run next dev"
    }
  }

How it works:
  1. Start the proxy once (HTTPS on port 443 by default, auto-elevates with sudo)
  2. Run your apps - they auto-start the proxy and register automatically
     (apps get a random port in the 4000-4999 range via PORT)
  3. Access via https://<name>.localhost
---

portless proxy - Manage the portless proxy server.

Usage:
  portless proxy start                Start the HTTPS proxy on port 443 (daemon)
  portless proxy start --no-tls       Start without HTTPS (port 80)
  portless proxy start --lan          Enable LAN mode (mDNS, .local TLD)
  portless proxy start --foreground   Start in foreground (for debugging)
  portless proxy start -p 1355        Start on a custom port (no sudo)
  portless proxy start --tld test     Use .test instead of .localhost
  portless proxy start --wildcard     Allow unregistered subdomains to fall back to parent
  portless proxy stop                 Stop the proxy

LAN mode (--lan):
  Makes services accessible from other devices on the same WiFi network
  via mDNS (.local domains). Useful for testing on real mobile devices.
  Auto-detects your LAN IP and follows changes automatically, or use
  --ip to pin one.
  Stopped LAN proxies keep LAN mode for the next start via proxy.lan.
  Use PORTLESS_LAN=0 for one start to switch back to .localhost mode.

---
No active routes.
Start an app with: portless <name> <command>
---

portless run - Infer project name and run through the proxy.

Usage:
  portless run [options] <command...>

Options:
  --name <name>          Override the inferred base name (worktree prefix still applies)
  --force                Kill the existing process and take over its route
  --app-port <number>    Use a fixed port for the app (skip auto-assignment)
  --help, -h             Show this help

Name inference (in order):
  1. package.json "name" field (walks up directories)
  2. Git repo root directory name
  3. Current directory basename

  Use --name to override the inferred name while keeping worktree prefixes.
  In git worktrees, the branch name is prepended as a subdomain prefix
  (e.g. feature-auth.myapp.localhost).

Examples:
  portless run next dev               # -> https://<project>.localhost
  portless run --name myapp next dev  # -> https://myapp.localhost
  portless run vite dev               # -> https://<project>.localhost
  portless run --app-port 3000 pnpm start

input.command:
SPIKE=/tmp/mfe-portless-spike-4

# Tiny HTTP echo server
cat > "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" <<'EOF'
const http=require('http');
const port=Number(process.env.PORT||process.argv[2]);
const label=process.argv[3]||process.env.LABEL||'?';
http.createServer((req,res)=>{
  res.writeHead(200,{'Content-Type':'text/plain'});
  res.end(`label=${label} port=${port} path=${req.url} host=${req.headers.host}\n`);
}).listen(port,'127.0.0.1',()=>console.error(`listening ${label}:${port}`));
EOF

# Start a fake "lightfast-app" from PRIMARY worktree via portless run
cd "$SPIKE/primary"
LABEL=primary-app portless run --name lightfast-app node "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" > /tmp/primary-app.log 2>&1 &
PRIMARY_APP_PID=$!
echo "primary app pid=$PRIMARY_APP_PID"
sleep 2

# Start a fake "lightfast-app" from SIBLING worktree (same --name, different worktree)
cd "$SPIKE/sibling"
LABEL=sibling-app portless run --name lightfast-app node "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" > /tmp/sibling-app.log 2>&1 &
SIBLING_APP_PID=$!
echo "sibling app pid=$SIBLING_APP_PID"
sleep 2

echo "--- portless list ---"
portless list

echo "--- primary log ---"
cat /tmp/primary-app.log | head -10
echo "--- sibling log ---"
cat /tmp/sibling-app.log | head -10

content.text:
**Critical finding**: `portless get <name>` IS worktree-aware. From primary it returns `lightfast-app.localhost`; from sibling it returns `fix-ui.lightfast-app.localhost`. This is a worktree-relative URL resolver primitive shipped by portless.

Now let me test whether we can pipe that into MFE without a wrapper script. Hypothesis: `MFE_CONFIG` env var built inline via shell substitution of `portless get` calls = zero-script per-worktree routing.

input.description: Add www apps for both worktrees and stage canonical microfrontends.json
Build per-worktree MFE_CONFIG inline using portless get

input.command:
SPIKE=/tmp/mfe-portless-spike-4
MFE_PKG="/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/node_modules/.pnpm/@vercel+microfrontends@2.3.2_@vercel+analytics@2.0.1_next@16.2.4_@opentelemetry+api@1.9_7e8377515d6d1392d856b874cb516849/node_modules/@vercel/microfrontends/dist/bin/cli.cjs"

# Helper: invocation that builds MFE_CONFIG inline + wraps proxy in portless on dynamic port
RUN_PROXY='portless run --name lightfast bash -c '"'"'
  CFG=$(node -e "
    const {execSync}=require(\"child_process\");
    const fs=require(\"fs\");
    const cfg=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(\"./microfrontends.json\",\"utf8\"));
    cfg.applications[\"lightfast-app\"].development.local=execSync(\"portless get lightfast-app\").toString().trim();
    cfg.applications[\"lightfast-www\"].development.local=execSync(\"portless get lightfast-www\").toString().trim();
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(cfg));
  ")
  export MFE_CONFIG="$CFG"
  export MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT="$PORT"
  exec node "'$MFE_PKG'" proxy --local-apps lightfast-app lightfast-www
'"'"''

# Boot primary's proxy
cd "$SPIKE/primary/apps/app"
eval "$RUN_PROXY" > /tmp/primary-mfe.log 2>&1 &
sleep 3

# Boot sibling's proxy
cd "$SPIKE/sibling/apps/app"
eval "$RUN_PROXY" > /tmp/sibling-mfe.log 2>&1 &
sleep 3

echo "=== portless list ==="
portless list

echo
echo "=== primary MFE log ==="
cat /tmp/primary-mfe.log | tail -25

echo
echo "=== sibling MFE log ==="
cat /tmp/sibling-mfe.log | tail -25

input.description: Find where MFE_CONFIG is actually consumed

content.text:
Critical finding: `MFE_CONFIG` env var is NOT read by `microfrontends proxy` — it only works for `MicrofrontendsServer.fromEnv()` (the runtime middleware path). The `proxy` CLI command goes through `LocalProxy.fromFile` → `infer()` → reads `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG` (file path), not `MFE_CONFIG` (inline JSON). So we *must* write a file.

Let me retest with `VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG` pointing at a generated per-worktree file built from `portless get`:

input.command:
SPIKE=/tmp/mfe-portless-spike-4
MFE_BIN="/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/node_modules/.pnpm/@vercel+microfrontends@2.3.2_@vercel+analytics@2.0.1_next@16.2.4_@opentelemetry+api@1.9_7e8377515d6d1392d856b874cb516849/node_modules/@vercel/microfrontends/dist/bin/cli.cjs"

# kill prior failed proxies
pkill -f "microfrontends/dist/bin/cli.cjs" 2>/dev/null
sleep 1

# Tiny resolver script — what would live in apps/app/scripts/mfe-config.cjs
cat > "$SPIKE/mfe-config.cjs" <<'NODE'
const {execSync}=require('child_process');
const fs=require('fs');
const path=require('path');
const c=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('./microfrontends.json','utf8'));
for (const name of Object.keys(c.applications)) {
  const url=execSync(`portless get ${name}`).toString().trim();
  if (c.applications[name].development) c.applications[name].development.local=url;
}
const out=`/tmp/mfe-${path.resolve('.').replace(/\W/g,'-')}.json`;
fs.writeFileSync(out, JSON.stringify(c, null, 2));
process.stdout.write(out);
NODE

# Boot primary's proxy (wrapped in portless, config built per-worktree from portless get)
cd "$SPIKE/primary/apps/app"
PRIMARY_CFG=$(node "$SPIKE/mfe-config.cjs")
echo "=== PRIMARY config: $PRIMARY_CFG ==="
cat "$PRIMARY_CFG"

VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG="$PRIMARY_CFG" portless run --name lightfast bash -c '
  export MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT="$PORT"
  exec node "'"$MFE_BIN"'" proxy --local-apps lightfast-app lightfast-www
' > /tmp/primary-mfe.log 2>&1 &
sleep 4

# Boot sibling's proxy
cd "$SPIKE/sibling/apps/app"
SIBLING_CFG=$(node "$SPIKE/mfe-config.cjs")
echo
echo "=== SIBLING config: $SIBLING_CFG ==="
cat "$SIBLING_CFG"

VC_MICROFRONTENDS_CONFIG="$SIBLING_CFG" portless run --name lightfast bash -c '
  export MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT="$PORT"
  exec node "'"$MFE_BIN"'" proxy --local-apps lightfast-app lightfast-www
' > /tmp/sibling-mfe.log 2>&1 &
sleep 4

echo
echo "=== portless list ==="
portless list

echo
echo "=== primary-mfe log (tail) ==="
tail -15 /tmp/primary-mfe.log

echo
echo "=== sibling-mfe log (tail) ==="
tail -15 /tmp/sibling-mfe.log
input.description: Run two MFE proxies wrapped in portless, each with its own per-worktree config built from portless get

content.text: **Spike 4 CONFIRMED end-to-end.** Concurrent multi-worktree `dev:full` works — both proxies running, each routing isolated to its own worktree's apps. Now let me clean up and present the findings.

input.command:

pkill -f "echo.cjs" 2>/dev/null
pkill -f "microfrontends/dist/bin/cli.cjs" 2>/dev/null
pkill -f "portless proxy" 2>/dev/null
sleep 2
rm -rf "$SPIKE" /tmp/mfe-*.json /tmp/primary-*.log /tmp/sibling-*.log 2>/dev/null

questions.question: Given the spike proves a file MUST be written, which shape do you want?

options.label: Tiny resolver at apps/app/scripts/mfe-config.cjs (Recommended)
options.description: ~10 lines of Node, sits next to microfrontends.json, called via `node ./scripts/mfe-config.cjs` in package.json. Discoverable, single-purpose, testable. Spike shape verbatim.

options.label: Inline node -e in package.json
options.description: Same logic squashed into one shell-escaped line in `apps/app/package.json#proxy`. No file added; harder to read; same behavior.

options.label: Commit microfrontends.json with portless URLs hardcoded
options.description: Bake `local: "https://lightfast-app.localhost:1355"` into the committed config. Works for primary worktree only; sibling worktrees route to primary's apps (the URL is the unprefixed primary registration). Breaks the concurrency requirement — listed for completeness.

options.description: ~10 lines of Node, sits next to microfrontends.json, called via `node ./scripts/mfe-config.cjs` in package.json. Discoverable, single-purpose, testable. Spike shape verbatim.

options.label: Inline node -e in package.json
options.description: Same logic squashed into one shell-escaped line in `apps/app/package.json#proxy`. No file added; harder to read; same behavior.

options.label: Commit microfrontends.json with portless URLs hardcoded
options.description: Bake `local: "https://lightfast-app.localhost:1355"` into the committed config. Works for primary worktree only; sibling worktrees route to primary's apps (the URL is the unprefixed primary registration). Breaks the concurrency requirement — listed for completeness.

answers.Given the spike proves a file MUST be wr: are you sure there no way to do this without a new file?

input.command:
SPIKE=/tmp/mfe-portless-spike-5
rm -rf "$SPIKE" && mkdir -p "$SPIKE/primary/apps/app" "$SPIKE/sibling/apps/app"
MFE_BIN="/Users/jeevanpillay/Code/@lightfastai/lightfast/node_modules/.pnpm/@vercel+microfrontends@2.3.2_@vercel+analytics@2.0.1_next@16.2.4_@opentelemetry+api@1.9_7e8377515d6d1392d856b874cb516849/node_modules/@vercel/microfrontends/dist/bin/cli.cjs"

# Restart portless daemon
portless proxy stop 2>/dev/null; sleep 1
portless proxy start -p 1355 > /dev/null 2>&1 &
sleep 2
portless list

# Set up two real worktrees
cd "$SPIKE"
git init -q primary && cd primary && git config user.email a@b.c && git config user.name a && echo '{"name":"primary"}' > package.json && git add . && git commit -qm init && git branch -m main
cd "$SPIKE/primary" && git worktree add -b fix-ui ../sibling -q
mkdir -p "$SPIKE/sibling/apps/app"

# Echo server
cat > "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" <<'EOF'
const http=require('http');
const port=Number(process.env.PORT||process.argv[2]);
const label=process.argv[3]||process.env.LABEL||'?';
http.createServer((req,res)=>{res.writeHead(200);res.end(`label=${label} port=${port} path=${req.url}\n`)}).listen(port,'127.0.0.1');
EOF

# Static microfrontends.json (committed, identical in both worktrees, NO `local` field)
cat > "$SPIKE/canonical.json" <<'EOF'
{
  "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/microfrontends.json",
  "applications": {
    "lightfast-app": { "development": { "fallback": "vercel.com" } },
    "lightfast-www": { "development": {}, "routing": [{ "paths": ["/docs/:path*"] }] }
  }
}
EOF
cp "$SPIKE/canonical.json" "$SPIKE/primary/apps/app/microfrontends.json"
cp "$SPIKE/canonical.json" "$SPIKE/sibling/apps/app/microfrontends.json"

# Boot fake apps
cd "$SPIKE/primary" && LABEL=primary-app portless run --name lightfast-app node "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" > /dev/null 2>&1 &
sleep 1
LABEL=primary-www portless run --name lightfast-www node "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" > /dev/null 2>&1 &
sleep 1
cd "$SPIKE/sibling" && LABEL=sibling-app portless run --name lightfast-app node "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" > /dev/null 2>&1 &
sleep 1
LABEL=sibling-www portless run --name lightfast-www node "$SPIKE/echo.cjs" > /dev/null 2>&1 &
sleep 2

portless list
echo
echo "=== TEST: process substitution as MFE config path (NO file written) ==="
echo "=== Primary: microfrontends proxy <(node -e '...portless get...') ==="
cd "$SPIKE/primary/apps/app"

portless run --name lightfast bash -c '
  export MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT="$PORT"
  exec node "'"$MFE_BIN"'" proxy <(node -e "
    const {execSync}=require(\"child_process\");
    const fs=require(\"fs\");
    const c=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(\"./microfrontends.json\",\"utf8\"));
    for (const n of Object.keys(c.applications)) {
      c.applications[n].development.local=execSync(\"portless get \"+n).toString().trim();
    }
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(c));
  ") --local-apps lightfast-app lightfast-www
' > /tmp/primary-procsub.log 2>&1 &
sleep 4

cd "$SPIKE/sibling/apps/app"
portless run --name lightfast bash -c '
  export MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT="$PORT"
  exec node "'"$MFE_BIN"'" proxy <(node -e "
    const {execSync}=require(\"child_process\");
    const fs=require(\"fs\");
    const c=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(\"./microfrontends.json\",\"utf8\"));
    for (const n of Object.keys(c.applications)) {
      c.applications[n].development.local=execSync(\"portless get \"+n).toString().trim();
    }
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(c));
  ") --local-apps lightfast-app lightfast-www
' > /tmp/sibling-procsub.log 2>&1 &
sleep 4

echo
echo "=== portless list ==="
portless list
echo
echo "=== primary proxy log (last 10) ==="
tail -10 /tmp/primary-procsub.log
echo
echo "=== sibling proxy log (last 10) ==="
tail -10 /tmp/sibling-procsub.log
echo
echo "=== Routing check ==="
curl -s -k https://lightfast.localhost:1355/sign-in
curl -s -k https://lightfast.localhost:1355/docs/x
curl -s -k https://fix-ui.lightfast.localhost:1355/sign-in
curl -s -k https://fix-ui.lightfast.localhost:1355/docs/x
echo
echo "=== Files in /tmp (no NEW files written by us) ==="
ls -la /tmp/mfe-*.json 2>/dev/null || echo "  no /tmp/mfe-*.json files exist"

content.text:
**Spike 5 CONFIRMED — it works with NO file written.**

```
Routing:
  primary /sign-in   → primary-app:4784    ✓
  primary /docs/x    → primary-www:4837    ✓
  sibling /sign-in   → sibling-app:4251    ✓
  sibling /docs/x    → sibling-www:4519    ✓

Files: zero /tmp/mfe-*.json, zero new files in either worktree
```

The trick: bash **process substitution** `<(...)` gives the MFE proxy a `/dev/fd/N` path (a kernel-managed pipe, not a real file). MFE's `fs.readFileSync(filePath)` reads it transparently. The committed `microfrontends.json` stays the single source of truth; per-worktree URLs are computed inline via `portless get` and piped to MFE.

Cleanup:

input.command:
pkill -f "echo.cjs" 2>/dev/null
pkill -f "microfrontends/dist/bin/cli.cjs" 2>/dev/null
pkill -f "portless proxy" 2>/dev/null
sleep 2
rm -rf /tmp/mfe-portless-spike-* /tmp/p.log /tmp/s.log /tmp/primary-* /tmp/sibling-* 2>/dev/null
echo "cleaned"

content.text:
## Final answer to "is there any way without a new file?"

**Yes — bash process substitution.** No `scripts/` file, no `/tmp/*.json` written, no `.gitignore` entry needed. The committed `microfrontends.json` stays the single source of truth.

The shape that fits in `apps/app/package.json#proxy`:

```jsonc
"proxy": "portless run --name lightfast bash -c 'export MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT=$PORT; exec microfrontends proxy <(node -e \"const{execSync}=require(\\\"child_process\\\");const c=JSON.parse(require(\\\"fs\\\").readFileSync(\\\"./microfrontends.json\\\",\\\"utf8\\\"));for(const n of Object.keys(c.applications))c.applications[n].development.local=execSync(\\\"portless get \\\"+n).toString().trim();process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(c));\") --local-apps lightfast-app lightfast-www'"
```

What this does, end-to-end:
1. `portless run --name lightfast` wraps the proxy → portless allocates a listen port, exposes at `lightfast.localhost` (primary) or `<branch>.lightfast.localhost` (sibling)
2. `MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT=$PORT` tells MFE to listen on portless's allocated port
3. `<(node -e ...)` opens a process-sub pipe; the inline Node reads `./microfrontends.json`, calls `portless get <name>` for each app (worktree-aware URL — already proven), injects them into `applications.*.development.local`, prints the merged JSON
4. MFE proxy reads the pipe via `fs.readFileSync`, treats it as its config
5. Apps + www are wrapped independently via `portless run --name lightfast-app/lightfast-www` — portless allocates per-worktree ports

Trade-offs:

- **Pro**: Zero new files. Zero `scripts/` directory. Zero generated artifacts. Vercel's `microfrontends.json` is untouched.
- **Pro**: Uses only Vercel/portless-shipped primitives — `portless get`, MFE CLI's `[filePath]` arg, `MFE_LOCAL_PROXY_PORT`.
- **Con**: One brutal line in `package.json` with three layers of escaping (JSON → bash → node `-e`).
- **Con**: Bash-only. macOS/Linux fine; Windows cmd would break (the plan's already macOS/Linux scope).
- **Con**: Debugging a misrouted request means re-running the inline Node by hand to see the resolved config — no file to `cat`.

Want me to update the plan with this shape (truly zero-file), or do you want a tiny extracted Node module despite it being one extra file?
